description: Derived from the Malay words for 'person of the forest,' Sumatran orangutans are almost exclusively arboreal, living among the trees of tropical rainforests.
habitats: Tropical and subtropical moist broadleaf forests
conservation_actions: Fighting wildlife crime
Saving orangutan forests
Mitigating human-orangutan conflict

description: The Bornean orangutan has a broader face, shorter beard, and is slightly darker in color compared to the Sumatran orangutan. It has three localized subspecies: Northwest, Northeast, and Central Bornean.
conservation_actions: Restoring habitat integrity
Addressing illegal killing and trade
Reducing human-orangutan conflicts
Panda CLICK! program

description: Torpedo-shaped with dark metallic blue backs, yellow sides, and a silver belly. They are highly migratory, breed year-round, and act as a top predator in the marine food chain.
conservation_actions: Advocating for stricter plans to recover depleted tuna stocks
Combating pirate fishing
Reducing bycatch
Marine Stewardship Council (MSC) certification
International Seafood Sustainability Foundation (ISSF) partnerships
Raising consumer awareness

description: Known for its mischievous smile and a level of intelligence comparable to a gorilla. It is the close cousin of the extinct Baiji dolphin and lives in the Yangtze River, the longest river in Asia.
conservation_actions: Reconnecting floodplain lakes to restore seasonal flows
Providing fishermen with alternative income generation methods
Stopping overfishing

description: The second-largest land mammal, also known as the square-lipped rhinoceros. Features a complex social structure and contains two genetically distinct subspecies: the near-extinct Northern white rhino and the recovering Southern white rhino.
habitats: Long and short grass savanna areas
conservation_actions: Strengthening local and international law enforcement
Effective public-private partnerships
Monitoring and protection
Rhino horn DNA analysis (RhoDIS)

description: The largest shark and largest fish alive today. They are gentle, transient plankton eaters with white spotted coloration unique to each individual like a human fingerprint.
conservation_actions: Scientific research using satellite tags and sonar
Photo-identification computer database tracking
Eco-tourism management and regulation
Tourist and boat operator education on codes of conduct

description: Extremely sociable polar mammals that live in pods. Known as the 'canary of the sea' due to their extensive vocalizations, they possess a flexible melon forehead capable of changing shape.
conservation_actions: Influencing Arctic shipping guidelines
Advocating for oil spill prevention and response
Community-based whale health monitoring
Strengthening the International Whaling Commission (IWC)
Raising awareness on underwater noise (Don’t Be a Buckethead initiative)

description: The largest animal on the planet with a heart the size of a car. They are the loudest animals on Earth, using low-frequency whistles reaching 188 decibels to communicate across hundreds of miles.
conservation_actions: Satellite tracking of migration and feeding routes
Establishing protected areas away from salmon farms and fisheries
Policy advocacy through the International Whaling Commission (IWC)

description: Known as the unicorn of the sea due to a long, spiraled tusk that can grow up to 10 feet. The tusk is an enlarged tooth with up to 10 million nerve endings, providing sensory capabilities and playing a role in male dominance.
conservation_actions: Satellite tagging to study feeding and reproductive routines
Improving whale protection via the International Whaling Commission (IWC)
Protecting whales from ocean noise (Don’t Be a Buckethead initiative)

description: A baleen whale with a hump and a ridge of sharp bumps instead of a dorsal fin. They are bottom-feeders that stay close to shore and are known for their incredibly long migrations of up to 12,000 miles round-trip. Affectionately called 'friendly ones' for approaching boats.
conservation_actions: Restricting seismic surveys
Urging the establishment of a gray whale sanctuary
Campaigning against offshore oil drilling platforms
Monitoring population size and health via photo identification and tissue sampling
Recording underwater communication
Pushing for stricter environmental standards for offshore projects

description: The world's fastest land mammal, reaching speeds of 60-70 mph. Characterized by narrow shoulder blades, long limbs, a long balancing tail, and semi-retractable claws. Unlike other big cats, they cannot roar; they chirp and purr instead.
conservation_actions: Protecting critical corridors and transboundary habitats
Addressing illegal trade via the Coalition to End Wildlife Trafficking Online
Publishing the Exotic Pet Suitability Guide
Researching and monitoring via camera traps, spoor surveys, and satellite collars

description: The smallest Asian elephant subspecies and the largest mammal on Borneo. Distinctly smaller than mainland elephants, they feature long tails that sometimes touch the ground, relatively large ears, and straight tusks.
conservation_actions: Satellite and radio collaring for tracking movement patterns
Creating reforested wildlife corridors through plantations
Sustainable forest management for timber production

description: A cultural icon in Asia that acts as a major seed disperser. They spend up to 19 hours a day feeding and produce about 220 pounds of dung daily while wandering areas up to 125 square miles.
conservation_actions: Protecting elephant habitat
Restoring critical habitats and biological corridors
Mitigating human-elephant conflict via patrols and community awareness
Equipping and supporting forest guards
Reconnecting protected areas to secure migratory routes

description: The largest and darkest of the Asian elephant subspecies, featuring distinct patches of depigmentation on its ears, face, trunk, and belly. Led by a matriarch, herds contain complex nursing and juvenile care units.
habitats: Tropical and subtropical moist broadleaf forests
conservation_actions: Protected under Sri Lankan law

description: Feeds on a variety of plants and deposits seeds to maintain healthy forest ecosystems. Half of its population has been lost in one generation due to extreme deforestation.
conservation_actions: Elephant Flying Squad to reduce human-elephant conflict
Securing healthy forests and declaring protected areas
Wildlife patrol units conducting antipoaching patrols
Collaborating with TRAFFIC to reduce illicit ivory markets

description: Smaller and more elusive than the savanna elephant, with oval-shaped ears and straighter, downward-pointing tusks. Known as the 'mega-gardener of the forest' due to their crucial role in germinating and dispersing the seeds of large rainforest trees.
conservation_actions: Creating employment in tourism and protected areas management
Promoting community-based fisheries as protein alternatives
Sangha Tri-national Anti-poaching Brigade cross-border patrols
Monitoring the Illegal Killing of Elephants (MIKE)
PAPECALF regional law enforcement network
Assessing ivory markets and reducing consumer demand
Coalition to End Wildlife Trafficking Online

description: The largest terrestrial animal on Earth, distinguished by very large ears that radiate heat. They form clans led by a female matriarch and contribute to savanna maintenance by reducing tree densities.
conservation_actions: Managing protected areas and migration corridors
Kavango Zambezi Transfrontier Conservation Area (KAZA) transboundary movement
Fostering ecotourism and joint-venture lodges
Using chili bombs, beehive fences, and noisemakers to deter elephants
Anti-poaching efforts
Coalition to End Wildlife Trafficking Online

description: One of the largest rodents in the world and a crucial 'ecosystem engineer' and 'keystone species'. They build dams that create wetlands, store carbon, prevent flooding, and improve water quality.
conservation_actions: Installing flow devices to prevent flooding damage
Protecting trees with metal fencing
Sustainable Ranching Initiative (SRI)
Building beaver dam analogs (low-tech process-based restoration)
Conserving the Rio Grande water supply

description: One of only 12 grassland birds endemic to the western Great Plains. They strictly nest in areas with sparse vegetation or bare ground, heavily relying on prairie dog towns.
threats: Conversion of native prairie for agriculture
conservation_actions: Working with researchers and landowners to understand habitat needs
Assessing how land management and climate change impact the bird

description: Exhibits the most highly evolved migration pattern of any known insect, traveling 1,200 to 2,800 miles. Possesses distinct orange-red wings with black veins and white spots. Larvae are entirely dependent on milkweed as their sole food source.
conservation_actions: Planting milkweed to support pollinators
Promoting sustainable forest management and tourism in Mexico
Supporting tree nurseries for income generation
Conducting multiyear field trials for non-neonic treated seeds
Restoring plowed grasslands back to pollinator habitat

description: The world's only oceangoing lizard, endemic to the Galápagos Islands where they are typically seen resting on rocky shores.
conservation_actions: Extending protected nesting zones
Installing educational signs for tourists and local communities
Building temporary fences to protect nesting areas

description: The world's largest known predatory fish. A top marine predator with a torpedo-shaped body that rips prey into mouth-sized pieces to swallow whole using 300 teeth.
conservation_actions: Research and monitoring of migration
Satellite tagging and tracking
Developing management plans for protected areas
Regulating tourism

status: Recovering in some regions / Declining in others
population: Varying by region (All-time low in mid-2000s, recovering in India/Nepal/Bhutan)
description: Found on the Asian mainland, encompassing Bengal, Malayan, Indochinese, and Amur populations. An apex predator that plays a critical role in ecosystem function; protecting one tiger effectively secures around 25,000 acres of forest.
threats: Black market demand for status symbols and decorative items
Prey loss (deer, wild boar, wild cattle)
conservation_actions: Enforcing zero tolerance for poaching via dedicated enforcement units
TRAFFIC collaboration to stop black market channel distribution
Deploying biogas technology for cooking stoves to reduce logging
Creating community employment and income through ecotourism
Securing continuous landscapes, conservation leases, and wildlife corridors

population: Varying / Holding on in remaining forest patches
description: Distinguished by heavy black stripes on an orange coat. They are the last remaining Sunda Island tigers, with Javan and Balinese cousins now extinct. Their presence serves as a vital indicator of forest health.
threats: Black market demand for tiger parts
Clearing for agriculture and oil palm
conservation_actions: TX2 global initiative to double wild tiger populations
Managing a 100,000-acre forest concession company in Thirty Hills for restoration
Influencing district and provincial land-use planning and zoning
Groundbreaking research using camera traps to map wildlife corridors
Campaigning to phase out tiger farms

description: An ancient, functionally blind river dolphin that originated in the Tethys Sea 50 million years ago. Relies entirely on echolocation to navigate and hunt in muddy river waters; serves as a vital indicator of river health.
threats: Pesticides from sugarcane and cotton crops
conservation_actions: Establishing Farmer Field Schools to reduce agricultural chemical runoff
Rescuing stranded dolphins from irrigation canals
Training fishermen to lead ecotourism and dolphin watch activities
Population monitoring and tracking movements via radio tags

description: Recognizable by a bulging forehead and short beak. Considered sacred by Khmer and Lao people, and serves as a crucial indicator for the overall health of the Mekong River.
habitats: Coastal areas in South and Southeast Asia
conservation_actions: Conducting population and ecology surveys
Habitat restoration and infrastructure improvements with corporate partners
CITES ban on international live trade
Developing community fishery management zones
Supporting alternative livelihoods (aquaculture, chicken raising, home-gardens)

description: The world's rarest marine mammal and most endangered cetacean. Recognized by a large dark ring around its eyes and dark patches on its lips. Extinction is imminent without a fully enforced gillnet ban.
threats: Illegal trade in totoaba swim bladders
conservation_actions: Developing and implementing vaquita-safe fishing technologies
Enforcing gillnet bans in the Upper Gulf of California
Retrieving lost or abandoned ghost gillnets
Acoustic monitoring to estimate populations
Bilateral conservation collaboration between the US and Mexico
Combating the illegal totoaba trade globally

description: Also known as the pink river dolphin or boto. It is a freshwater cetacean that serves as a vital indicator of the health of the Amazon and Orinoco river basins.
threats: Mercury pollution from small-scale gold mining
Deliberately killed for use as bait in catfish fisheries
conservation_actions: Protecting river dolphin habitat in the Amazon region
Supporting river dolphin surveys to determine status
Researching the impact of dams on size and dispersal of populations

description: The most threatened of the five rhino species. They have a dusky grey color, a single horn up to 10 inches, and loose skin folds that give the appearance of armor plating.
conservation_actions: Intensive population monitoring

description: The largest sea turtle species, named for its leather-like shell rather than a hard shell. Highly migratory across the Atlantic and Pacific Oceans, where it consumes large numbers of jellyfish.
threats: Fisheries bycatch (shrimp trawl nets, longline hooks, gillnets)
Hunting and killing of adult turtles
conservation_actions: Working with fisheries to switch to turtle-friendly circle hooks
Smart Gear international competition to solve bycatch
Using satellite telemetry devices to track migratory routes
Establishing marine protected areas (MPAs)
Patrolling nesting beaches and equipping local conservationists
Reducing turtle and egg consumption through community awareness
Creating ecotourism opportunities and alternative livelihoods

status: Least concern (Globally as of 2025, though some regional populations remain Vulnerable/Endangered)
description: One of the largest sea turtles and the only herbivorous species. Named for the green color of their fat (from their diet of seagrass and algae), not their shells. They act as the 'lawnmowers' of the ocean, maintaining highly productive seagrass beds.
threats: Hunting for meat, medicine, and traditional ceremonies
Climate change (altering sand temperatures affecting hatchling sex)
conservation_actions: Working with fisheries to adopt turtle-friendly circle hooks
Smart Gear competition to innovate bycatch solutions
Establishing Marine Protected Areas (MPAs)
Monitoring and patrolling turtle nesting beaches
Using innovative DNA-based tools like Shellbank
Blue Corridors for Turtles tracking
Satellite tracking migration patterns
TRAFFIC monitoring of illegal trade

population: Most abundant of all sea turtles
description: Named for the olive green hue of its shell. While currently the most abundant sea turtle, they are highly vulnerable because they rely on mass nesting events at a very small number of specific beaches.
threats: Climate change (warmer temperatures disrupting sex ratios)
Warmer sea surface temperatures destroying foraging grounds
Severe storms and sea level rise damaging nests
conservation_actions: Establishing Marine Protected Areas (MPAs)
Monitoring and patrolling nesting beaches
Raising awareness on sea level rise and providing shade for nests
Working with fisheries to adopt turtle-friendly circle hooks
Running the Smart Gear international competition to solve bycatch
Satellite tracking movements to prevent fisheries interactions
Studying climate change effects to build ecosystem resilience

description: Named for their large heads and powerful jaw muscles that crush hard-shelled prey. They act as moving micro-ecosystems, with up to 100 species of small plants and animals recorded living on a single loggerhead's shell.
threats: Fisheries bycatch (shrimp trawl nets, longline hooks, fishing gillnets)
conservation_actions: Working with fisheries to adopt turtle-friendly circle hooks
Smart Gear international competition to innovate bycatch solutions
Tracking turtle movements with satellite devices
Establishing Marine Protected Areas (MPAs)
Monitoring and patrolling turtle nests
Equipping local turtle conservationists
Fostering ecotourism opportunities and alternative livelihoods

description: Named for their narrow, pointed beak and patterned, overlapping shell scales. They play a vital role in coral reef health by extracting sponges, which allows reef fish better access to feed.
threats: Loss of nesting and feeding habitats
conservation_actions: Working with fisheries to adopt circle hooks and turtle excluder devices
Smart Gear international competition to solve bycatch
Satellite tracking movements to prevent fisheries interactions
Establishing Marine Protected Areas (MPAs)
Monitoring and patrolling turtle nests
Addressing the wildlife trade through TRAFFIC
Developing alternative livelihoods and ecotourism

description: The largest bear in the world and the Arctic's top predator. Meaning 'sea bear', they are heavily dependent on sea ice for traveling, hunting seals, resting, and creating maternal dens.
threats: Climate change and loss of sea ice habitat
Offshore ship traffic and oil spills
conservation_actions: Advancing policies to reduce carbon emissions and promote renewable energy
Supporting locally led initiatives to reduce human-bear conflict
Monitoring populations using satellite telemetry ear tags and environmental DNA (eDNA)
Incorporating Traditional Ecological Knowledge (TEK) from Indigenous peoples
Opposing unsustainable oil and gas development
Protecting maternal denning areas and movement corridors
Regulating hunting via the International Agreement on the Conservation of Polar Bears

description: A bear with a distinctive black and white coat that serves as the universal symbol for conservation. It subsists almost entirely on bamboo and features enlarged wrist bones functioning as opposable thumbs.
threats: Accidental capture in snares set for other species
conservation_actions: Establishing over 50 panda reserves
Enacting the Wildlife Protection Act (1988)
Assisting and influencing policy-level conservation decisions
Generating sustainable economic benefits through ecotourism

description: One of North America's most endangered mammals and its only native ferret species. Rediscovered in 1981 after being thought extinct. Their survival is entirely dependent on prairie dogs and their colonies for food, shelter, and raising young.
conservation_actions: Captive breeding and reintroductions
Cloning
Habitat protection
Relocating prairie dogs to increase ferret habitat
Mitigating sylvatic plague and participating in oral vaccine research
Collaborating with tribal communities and private landowners

weight: Slightly larger than a domestic cat
description: A highly skillful and acrobatic arboreal mammal with thick russet fur, a bear-like body, and a long, bushy tail used for balance and winter warmth. Its name derives from the Nepali word 'ponya', meaning bamboo-eater.
threats: Clearing of forest home (loss of nesting trees and bamboo)
Accidental capture in traps meant for other animals (wild pigs, deer)
Illegal trade of fur caps and hats
conservation_actions: Monitoring populations and habitat across India, Nepal, and Bhutan
Evaluating reintroduction feasibility in Sikkim
Raising community awareness with farmers, students, and monks
Enforcing strict anti-poaching legal fines ($1,000 and/or 10 years jail)
Manufacturing yak dung briquettes as an alternative fuel source to prevent logging
Developing community-based tourism packages for alternative income

population: Unknown (Likely fewer than 100, possibly single digits)
description: Often called the Asian unicorn, it is one of the rarest large mammals on Earth, discovered only in 1992. Known by its two parallel spindle horns and white facial markings, it has not been confirmed in the wild since 2013 and cannot survive in traditional captivity.
threats: Growing demand for wild meat and traditional medicine
conservation_actions: Intensive camera trapping networks
eDNA stream surveys to detect surviving populations
Establishing saola reserves and improving community-based forest management
Capacity building for government and community rangers
Developing a specialized breeding center for potential future captures

description: The most common marine mammal in the Galápagos. They haul out in large colonies on rocks and shores, but frequently clash with local fishing communities when resting on boats.
threats: Climate change and El Niño altering ocean currents and fish prey abundance
Human-wildlife conflict (severe injuries from barbed wire on fishing boats)
conservation_actions: Designing and installing dedicated floating bases for sea lions to rest
Building a primary care nursery for injured individuals
Conducting environmental education and community awareness sessions
Implementing the 2025–2030 San Cristóbal Island Management Plan

description: Also known as the 'polar wolf' or 'white wolf'. It is the only wolf subspecies that still inhabits the entirety of its original range, thanks to the extreme isolation of its Arctic tundra habitat.
threats: Reduction in prey availability (musk ox and Arctic hare declines)
conservation_actions: Combating climate change impacts through global advocacy
Preserving biodiversity in the Arctic region
Monitoring habitat health via government and community partnerships

description: A primarily carnivorous mammal that lives inland in the tundra. They are heavily dependent on lemming population cycles for survival and act as a vital indicator of Arctic ecosystem stability.
threats: Climate change (reduced sea ice, melting permafrost, rising sea levels)
conservation_actions: Mitigating the effects of climate change
Protecting fragile Arctic ecosystems
Monitoring population health to ensure ecosystem safety

description: The 'ghost of the mountains,' this elusive big cat has evolved to thrive in high-altitude, rocky Himalayan environments. It is a top predator and a vital indicator of mountain ecosystem health and freshwater reservoir stability.
habitats: Asia (12 countries: Afghanistan, Bhutan, China, India, Kazakhstan, Kyrgyzstan, Mongolia, Nepal, Pakistan, Russia, Tajikistan, Uzbekistan)
threats: Climate change (loss of alpine habitat and prey)
Poaching for illegal wildlife trade (fur, bones, body parts)
Retaliatory killings due to livestock conflict
conservation_actions: Mobile antipoaching activities
Partnering with e-commerce companies to end online wildlife trafficking
Installing predator-proof livestock pens
Implementing community-based livestock insurance plans
National population assessments (e.g., Bhutan, India, Mongolia)
Integrating eDNA sampling and traditional ecological knowledge

description: Sluggish, long-armed tree-dwellers of Central and South American tropical rainforests. They have exceptionally low metabolic rates and are surprisingly excellent swimmers.
conservation_actions: Building canopy bridges
Encouraging sustainable forestry
Global Forest & Trade Network (GFTN) for responsible products
Amazon Region Protected Areas (ARPA) initiative

scientific_name: Manis spp. / Phataginus spp. / Smutsia spp.
description: The world's only scaly mammal. Solitary and primarily nocturnal, they are famous for curling into an armored ball when threatened. They act as essential forest and grassland regulators.
conservation_actions: Co-convening the Coalition to End Wildlife Trafficking Online
Lobbying for strong national laws and enforcement
Strengthening legislation in China (removing scales from pharmacopoeia)
Reducing market demand in China and Viet Nam
Blocking illegal online listings through tech company partnerships

status: Varies by species (Emperor penguin now Endangered as of April 2026)
description: Flightless seabirds that 'fly' underwater. They serve as a critical sentinel species; their population health, particularly that of Antarctic species, directly mirrors the status of sea ice and ocean food webs.
conservation_actions: Establishing marine protected areas (MPAs)
Implementing specialized management plans (e.g., Galápagos San Cristóbal Plan)
Fishery management to prevent competition for prey
Satellite remote sensing for population monitoring
